content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Nancy Diamond 
City Attorney 
City of Arcata 
822 G Street, Suite 3 
Arcata, CA 95521 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-143 
Dear Ms. Diamond: 
October 8, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Arcata City Councilmember 
Michael Winkler regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act") and Government Code Section 1090. 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), and Section 1090, not 
under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, 
including Public Contract Code. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Humboldt County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember Winkler participate in City of Arcata ("City") decisions related to the 
Creek Side Annexation Project due to his financial interest in his Redwood Energy consulting 
services business and its client, DANCO Communities, the applicant for the Project? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-143 
Page No. 2 
No. Under the Act, Councilmember Winkler has a source of income interest in DANCO to 
the extent that his pro rata share of income received by Redwood Energy from DANCO amounts to 
$500 or more in the 12 months prior to a decision. He may not participate in any such decisions 
involving DANCO, as discussed below. Under Section 1090 Councilmember Winkler has a remote 
interest, as a supplier of services to DANCO, in the development agreement between the City and 
DANCO. The City Council may act on this agreement; however, Councilmember Winkler must 
recuse himself from this process. 
FACTS PRESENTED BY THE REQUESTOR 
Councilmember Winkler has been an Arcata City Council member since 2008, with his 
most recent term commencing in December 2016. He co-owns a consulting services partnership 
called "Redwood Energy." This business provides energy consumption and production modeling 
and analyses to building project developers. These analyses are required to design buildings in 
compliance with statutory energy efficiency standards and to justify financial incentives (for 
example, tax credits) if performance exceeds statutory standards. Councilmember Winkler receives 
a fixed annual salary from Redwood Energy. Councilmember Winkler regularly performs energy 
analyses for specific DANCO projects located both inside and outside of the City on a project-by-
project basis and has done so since 2010. 
DANCO Communities ("DANCO") has applied to the city for an annexation, General Plan 
amendment, zoning amendment, minor subdivision, major subdivision, planned development 
permit, and design review permit for the "Creek Side Annexation Project." This Project would 
include the development of single-family residences, a senior assisted living with memory care 
facility, and senior-restricted neighborhood cottage units. The application is pending before the 
Arcata Planning Commission but has not been reviewed by the Arcata City Council. The Planning 
Commission's decision-making authority for this project is limited to making a recommendation to 
the City Council. 
DANCO is a collection of separately formed, development-oriented companies that provide 
building construction services, housing development projects, planning and development services, 
residential building, property management, and assisted living management and development. 
DANCO' s projects are based throughout the Northwest; however, many are located within the City 
and require decision-making by the City Council. The City and its former Redevelopment Agency 
have provided "partnership" financial support to many DANCO projects through State of California 
Community Development Block Grants (CDBG) and Home programs. No City financial support 
will be provided to the Creekside Annexation Project. 
Councilmember Winkler performs all work on DANCO projects located in the City for free, 
and DANCO pays Redwood Energy for Councilmember Winkler's services performed on projects 
located outside of the City. During calendar year 2018, Redwood Energy received approximately 


File No. A-19-143 
Page No. 3 
15% of its gross revenue from DANCO, and Councilmember Winkler anticipates that Redwood 
Energy will receive 25% of its gross revenue from DANCO in calendar year 2019. 
The City Council must take numerous actions for project approval including negotiating and 
entering into a development agreement with DANCO pursuant to Government Code §65864 et seq., 
through which DANCO will agree to pay certain fees for traffic, wastewater treatment, and water 
storage impacts. Your research indicates that this development agreement is a contract for purposes 
of Section 1090. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103 defines 
financial interests to include, as relevant to these facts: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth $2,000 or more; or is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
• 
Any source of income, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
Councilmember Winkler has a business entity and source of income interest in his business, 
Redwood Energy. Additionally, under Section 82030, "income" to an official "also includes a pro 
rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, 
directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." You confirmed by email that 
Councilmember Winkler has an ownership interest of 10-percent or more of Redwood Energy. 
Therefore, Councilmember Winkler also has an interest in DANCO as a source of income, to the 
extent that his pro rata share of income received by Redwood Energy amounts to $500 or more in 
the 12 months prior to a decision. 
Reasonably Foreseeable and Material Financial Effect 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to 
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision; and an 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. 


File No. A-19-143 
Page No. 4 
Under the materiality standard for a source of income interest, the financial effect is material 
where the source of income is a named party in, or the subject of the decision, including as an 
applicant or contracting party.2 (Regulation 18702.3(a)(l).) DANCO is the named party and 
applicant for the Creek Side Annexation Project decisions. Councilmember .Winkler will have a 
prohibitive financial interest to the extent that his pro rata share of income received by Redwood 
Energy from DANCO equals $500 or more in the 12 months prior to a decision. He may not 
participate in these decisions and must recuse himself pursuant to the requirements of Regulation 
18707.3 
Section 1090 
The City's DANCO application decisions include the negotiation and approval of a 
development agreement. As you note in your request, development agreements that involve 
negotiations, consideration and mutual assent are contracts for purposes of Section 1090, and thus 
this type of decision requires a separate analysis under this body oflaw. (See· 78 Ops. Cal. Atty. 
Gen. 230 (1995) [a development agreement constitutes a contract for purposes of Section 1090]; 
and Ansolabehere Advice Letter, No. A-17-160a [ discussion of land use decisions that are 
regulatory as opposed to contractual in nature.]) 
: 
S_ection 1090 states, "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, 
and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in 
their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Under Section 1090, 
"the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest.'' (People 
v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than 
remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided 
allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 
569.) 
An officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's 
contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the 
contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, at p. 649.) As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to 
one member of a governing body of a public entity, the entire governing body is precluded from 
entering into the contract. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) 
The phrase "financially interested" broadly encompasses anything that would tie a public 
official's fortunes to the existence of a public contract. ( Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla 
2 Where the source of income is a business entity, we also look to the materiality standard set forth in 
Regulation 18702.1. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Regulation 18702.l(a)(l) similarly finds materiality where the entity is 
a named party in, or the subject of, the decision, including where the entity initiates the proceeding by filing an 
application with the official's agency. Please note, the Commission repealed former Regulations 18702.3 and adopted a 
new version at its meeting on July 23, 2019, with an effective date of October 6, 2019. The letter applies the new 
version of this regulations. 
3 In light of the conclusion that Councilmember Winkler may not take part in the decisions under Section 1090 
and where it appears he is also prohibited from participating in decisions under the Act based upon his interest in 
DANCO as a source of income to Redwood Energy, we do not further analysis the potential financial effect on 
Councilmember Winkler's interests in Redwood Energy as a business entity and source of income. 


File No. A-19-143 
Page No. 5 
(2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323, 1335.) The prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as 
direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of 
pecuniary gain. A financial interest may include a board member's prospect of future business 
opportunities related to the contract or his desire to maintain a favorable ongoing relationship with 
the contracting party. (86 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 187, 189 (2003).) Here, Councilmember Winkler has 
a financial interest in DANCO due to his business' long-term and on-going relationship with 
DANCO. While his practice is to work on DANCO projects within the City's jurisdiction without a 
fee, Councilmember Winkler expects DANCO to account for 25 percent of his business's gross 
revenues in 2019. Not charging a fee on work for DANCO in particular situations does not alter his 
financial interest in the business relationship and prospect of future gain. We note that services 
provided pro bono may "still produce economic gains, goodwill, or prestige" for the public 
official's business. (86 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 138, 138-141 (2003).) 
The Legislature has expressly defined certain "remote interests" and "noninterests" that do 
not come within the prohibition of Section 1090. The primary remote interest requiring examination 
is that of Section 1091 (b )(8), where the interest is that of a supplier of goods and services to the 
contracting party by the public officer for at least five years prior to his or her election to office. 
Thus, an official would have a financial interest in a contract entered into by a government entity if 
he or she provides services to the party contracting with that government entity, but only a remote 
interest if those services have been provided for at least five years prior to election to the officer's 
most recent term, as opposed to the initial term of office. (86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 187, 191 supra.) 
This remote interest is applicable to these facts as Councilmember Winkler has supplied consulting 
services to DANCO for six years prior to the commencement of his current term of office in 2016. 
Pursuant to the requirements of Section 1091(a) and (c), Councilmember Winkler must disclose his 
remote interest in the City Council's official records, and the Council may proceed to negotiate the 
development agreement without any influence or participation by Councilmember Winkler. 
Additionally, we note that Councilmember Winkler must leave the room for the duration of 
the discussion and the vote on the development agreement as well as the other Creek Side 
Annexation Project decisions, to comply with the Act's conflict of interest recusal requirements. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

services business and its client, DANCO Communities, the applicant for the Project?

Under Section 1090 Councilmember Winkler has a remote interest, as a supplier of services to DANCO, in the development agreement between the City and DANCO. The City Council may act on this agreement; however, Councilmember Winkler must recuse himself from this process. FACTS PRESENTED BY THE REQUESTOR Councilmember Winkler has been an Arcata City Council member since 2008,
